sections.title: Introduction to Modal Verbs
sections.description: Modal verbs are used to express an attitude toward an action, such as ability, necessity, permission, or desire. They modify the main action of the sentence.

sections.title: Conjugation Rules
sections.description: There are two critical rules for conjugating modal verbs in German: Vowel Change and the 1st & 3rd Person Rule.

rules: Vowel Change: Most modal verbs change their stem vowel in the singular forms (ich, du, er/sie/es).
The 1st & 3rd Person Rule: The forms for ich and er/sie/es are always identical and do not take an ending.

sections.title: Sentence Architecture
sections.description: In a standard German sentence with a modal verb, follow this specific order:

structure: Position 2: The conjugated Modal Verb goes here.
The End: The Main Verb (the action) stays in its basic form (Infinitive) and moves to the very end.
content.formula: Subject + Modal Verb (Conjugated) + Objects/Adverbs + Main Verb (Infinitive)

sections.title: Usage Examples
sections.description: Learn how modal verbs are used in different contexts.

examples.german: Ich kann sehr gut Fußball spielen.
examples.english: I can play football very well.
One is not allowed to smoke here.

examples.german: Der Arzt sagt, ich soll im Bett bleiben.
examples.english: The doctor says I should stay in bed.
We want to travel to Germany.

sections.title: Advanced Note: Omission of Main Verbs
sections.description: In spoken German, if the action is obvious from the context (usually verbs of movement like gehen or fahren), the main verb at the end can be omitted.
